event.text: # Project Management Plan Update —

MAAP-1

**Document Control**
Program: AetherForge
MAAP-1 Tandem-Rotor Heavy-Lift Autonomous Helicopter
Issuing Organization: Eurus Systems Program Management

Office
Document Type: Program Management Plan (PMP) Update — Engineering

Change Assessment
Change Subject: Proposed Replacement of Aluminum Main Rotor Hub with Titan

ium Design
Contract Type: Firm Fixed Price (FFP)
Total Contract

Value: $4,128M
Update Classification: Class I Engineering Change —

Full CCB Review Required
Prepared By: Program Manager,
with inputs from Cost & Margin, Manufacturing, Supply

Chain, Change Impact, and Risk & Schedule functional leads
Distribution
: Program Director, Chief Engineer, VP Operations, VP Finance

, Program Executive Officer, Change Control Board Members

---

## 1. Introduction

### 1.1 Purpose of This Update

This Project Management Plan (PMP) Update formally
documents the programmatic response to the proposed engineering change to replace the aluminum main rotor hub with a titanium design on
the MAAP-1 AetherForge program. The update captures the integrated impact of this proposed
change across all ten program management domains, synthesizes findings
from specialized functional analyses conducted across the full program document
baseline, and establishes the authoritative management framework within which the Change

Control Board (CCB) will evaluate, approve, conditionally approve, or reject the change.

This document
supplements and partially supersedes relevant sections of the baseline PMP. All
sections herein reflect the state of analysis as of the assessment date and shall be updated upon CCB disposition

of the Engineering Change Proposal (ECP).

### 1.2 Background and Change Rat

ionale

The proposed change seeks to replace the main rotor hub material from aluminum (assumed
baseline for change proposal purposes) with Ti-6Al-4V,
driven by a fatigue life improvement objective. The primary technical
motivation is sound: Ti-6Al-4V exhibits a genuine fatigue endurance limit — approximately
60 ksi at 10⁷ cycles compared
to aluminum 7075-T7351's effective fatigue limit of approximately
22 ksi — making titanium substantially superior for a component that experiences
continuous, high-cycle loading at N/rev
and higher harmonics throughout every flight hour.
The rotor hub is the highest-cycle fatigue component on the aircraft, and improved
fatigue life translates directly into extended inspection intervals, higher fleet availability

, and reduced lifecycle cost.

However, a critical preliminary finding fundament
ally reframes the analysis: the current program Bill of Materials (BOM, AF
) already specifies the Main Rotor Hub Assembly as Ti-6Al-4V
Forging, AMS 4928 at $485,
000 per unit with a Long Lead (LL) designation. The
Blade Grip Assembly (AF-MAAP1-RS-1003-00, five
units per aircraft) is similarly specified as Ti-6Al-4V at $95,000 per set. This

finding generates three possible interpretations, each with distinct programmatic consequences:

**Interpretation A —
Configuration Management Discrepancy:** The BOM has been updated to reflect a titanium hub that has
not been formally authorized through the CCB process. The BOM is ahead of the
change control system, constituting a configuration management failure requiring immediate

corrective action.

**Interpretation B — Redesign Within Material Baseline:** The current titanium hub design, while correctly
specified as Ti-6Al-4V, does not achieve the fatigue life objectives required by the program's Key
Performance Parameters (KPPs). The change proposes a geometric redesign of the hub forging to exploit titan
ium's material properties more fully — a new dash number, new for
ging envelope, revised lug geometry — while retaining the same all

oy.

**Interpretation C — Legacy Aluminum Design in Active Drawing Tree
:** The BOM reflects a forward-priced optimistic baseline,
but the actual current design drawings still reference an aluminum hub that has not been formally superseded. The cost

baseline may therefore not reflect actual procurement intent.

**
This PMP Update proceeds on the assumption that Interpretation B or C represents the operative
scenario**, as these carry the most significant programmatic consequences.
Interpretation A (BOM is current and the change is already accomplished
) would substantially reduce the scope of action required; however, it would
not eliminate the configuration management corrective actions documented herein.
The CCB must resolve which interpretation applies before any design or
procurement action proceeds. Until that determination is made, this

document treats the proposed change as a live engineering change requiring full evaluation.

### 1.3 Governance Authority
The main rotor hub is explicitly designated a common Green Aircraft configuration item
under Program Requirements Baseline (PRB) Section 5.1,
which enumerates the rotor system, transmissions, drive train
, and rotor blades as components shared identically across all three aircraft variants
: MAAP-1F (firefighting), MAAP-1I (ISR), and MAAP-1

C (cargo). As a consequence:

- Any deviation
from common configuration status requires a formal waiver approved by the Chief Engineer and the Program

Executive Officer.
- The impact assessment submitted in support of that waiver must
explicitly address cost, schedule, and logistics footprint across

all three variants.
- The change must be processed exclusively through the Program CCB, ch

aired by the Program Manager.
- No design authority action,
procurement commitment, or supplier direction may be issued prior

to CCB disposition.

The proposed change is classified as a **Class I Engineering Change** under
the program's change classification framework: it involves
new tooling fabrication, supplier qualification or re-qualification, multi
-variant impacts, and cost impact estimated to exceed $500,000 by
several orders of magnitude. Class I changes require Program Manager approval at minimum and,
given the financial magnitude documented herein, require escalation to VP

level and formal management reserve authorization.

### 1.4 Scope of This

PMP Update

This update addresses the following program management domains:

- Project scope

definition and boundary conditions for the change
- Milestone and schedule baseline impacts, including critical

path analysis
- Change management process, governance, and decision authorities
- Cost management, including E
AC revision, management reserve assessment, and FFP contract exposure
- Risk management, including new and elevated risks requiring immediate register action

- Procurement and supply chain management, including supplier qualification, lead time, and make/buy implications
- Quality management, including qualification

testing, inspection, and airworthiness certification
- Staffing and resource management, including engineering capacity and specialized

workforce requirements
- Overall assessment and recommended path forward for CCB decision

---

## 2. Project

Scope

### 2.1 Baseline Scope Context

The MAAP-1 program scope encompasses the design, development, qualification
, and production of a tandem-rotor heavy-lift autonomous helicopter across three variants (MAAP-1F,
MAAP-1I, MAAP-1C) with a production baseline of 183 units under the current
FFP contract. The program is structured around a common Green
Aircraft platform — a shared structural and dynamic core — with variant
-specific mission system overlays. The rotor system is the highest-value and
highest-complexity element of the Green Aircraft, representing approximately $
1.235M per aircraft in procurement cost

for the main rotor assembly alone.

The program's integrated master
schedule (IMS) operates on a 92-month baseline with zero float
on the critical path. The rotor system development cycle is explicitly identified
as a 54-month critical path item. The program is currently approaching
CDR (PM 42), with Manufacturing Development (Phase
4) beginning December 2027, DTA-
1 fabrication scheduled for July 2029, and First Flight targeted

for December 2029.

### 2.2 Scope of the Proposed Change

The proposed change, as analyzed
under Interpretation B (the operative scenario for this PMP Update), constitutes a redesigned
titanium hub forging with improved fatigue geometry — a new forging envelope, revised hub
lug geometry, modified bearing retention features, and updated structural cross-sections
designed to exploit Ti-6Al-4V's superior fatigue allowables. This is not

a simple material substitution; it is a major design change that affects:

**Primary

Scope Elements:**
- Complete structural redesign of the Main Rotor Hub Assembly (AF-MAAP1-RS-1001-00), generating

a new part number (AF-MAAP1-RS-1001-01 or successor dash number)
- New

Ti-6Al-4V forging die design and fabrication for the revised hub geometry
- Full
building-block qualification test program (coupon,

element, component, full-scale) using the new design
- Updated fatigue and
damage tolerance analysis using titanium material allowables and

new forging geometry
- Revised machining program, tooling, and process qualification for the new hub geometry
- Updated First Article Inspection (FAI) requirements per AS9102

for the new configuration
- Supplier qualification or re-qualification actions for the forging

and machining supply chain

**Secondary Scope Elements Requiring Assessment

:**
- Galvanic compatibility review and potential material upgrades for
the Scissors Assembly (AF-MAAP1-RS-1011-00, currently Al
7075-T7351, $28,000) and Pitch Control
Horn assemblies (AF-MAAP1-RS-1004-00, Al 7075-T7351, $18,500
× 5 per aircraft), both of which are in direct

structural contact with the titanium hub
- Thermal expansion coefficient mismatch analysis for the elastomeric bearing interfaces
(titanium CTE approximately 5.0 µin/in/
°F versus aluminum at 13.1 µin/in/°F), with
potential revision of bearing preload, retention fitting design, and replacement

procedures
- Rotor dynamics re-analysis for the revised hub mass and stiffness properties, including blade
natural frequency verification against the 1.15/
rev ± 0.05 specification and ground/air reson

ance margin assessment
- Vibration survey re-baseline against the ≤0.50

in/sec RMS specification at rotor hubs
- Updated Fault
Tree Analysis (FTA) for rotor speed governing and HAZ-016 (
rotor overspeed/structural failure) to reflect revised

material properties and structural margins
- Rotor track and balance re-demonstration to the

±0.5 in blade tip tracking tolerance
- Transmissions
interface loads review for any changes in mast load path
stiffness resulting from titanium's higher elastic modulus (~116 GPa versus

aluminum's ~69 GPa)

**Out of Scope — Unless Secondary Analysis Reveals Necessity

:**
- Main transmission redesign (RISK-T-002 contingency monitor

only)
- Tail Rotor Hub (AF-MAAP1-RS-2001-00,
currently Al 7075-T7351 — separate CCB action if warran

ted)
- Rotor blade design changes (blade grips already specified as Ti-6Al-4V;

blade bodies unaffected)
- Avionics, autonomous systems, or mission

system changes

### 2.3 Common Configuration Boundary Condition

Because the main rotor hub is a Green
Aircraft common component, every element of scope described above applies
simultaneously and identically to all three variants (MAAP-1F, MAAP-1I, MAAP-1C). There
is no mechanism within current program policy to implement a material change on
one variant without applying it to all, absent a waiver from the Chief Engineer and Program Executive Officer. The future
MAAP-1N (Naval) variant should also be assessed for hub design
compatibility at this time, as the naval environment's
salt-air corrosion profile makes titanium's inherent corrosion resistance particularly advant
ageous and may justify earlier commitment to the titanium design for that derivative program

.

### 2.4 BOM Discrepancy — Mandatory Scope Item

Regardless
of which interpretation governs the operative scenario, the following configuration management action is mandatory
scope for this change action and must be completed before any downstream program

action:

An emergency engineering and finance review must be convened within ten
business days of this PMP Update's issuance to formally determine whether the current cost
baseline reflects titanium or aluminum hub pricing. The outcome of that review determines
the class and scope of the change action. If the BOM is already titan
ium-priced and the design is mature, the change request should be closed with a configuration
management discrepancy notice. If the BOM is aluminum-era pricing carried forward, the cost baseline must be corrected as
a prerequisite to any financial decision. This review is a

prerequisite gate that cannot be bypassed.

---

## 3. Milestone List & Schedule Baseline

### 3.1

Baseline IMS Context and Zero-Float Criticality

The MAAP-
1 IMS operates on a 92-month critical path with documented zero days of float. The rotor system is on the critical path throughout
the development phase. The structural test and qualification sequence

is the primary driver of schedule risk introduced by this change, as follows:

MS Reference | Baseline Date |
|---|---|---|

Testing Complete | WBS 2.2.3

-Lead Item Procurement Award | WBS 4.5.2 | 40

weeks prior to DTA-1 |

Structural Test Article) Delivery | WBS 4.6.1 | 15

, Critical Path) | TEMP Phase 3 | PM 47

–53 (24 weeks) |

Qualification Review | TEMP PM 54 | PM 54 |

### 3.2 Schedule Impact of Proposed Change

The schedule
impact of the proposed change is direct, material, and currently unmitigated. The following

table summarizes the key impact points:

| Activity | Current Duration | Change Impact | Estimated Slip

|
|---|---|---|---|
| Materials Selection & Testing (WBS 2.2.3, Ti
coupon characterization) | 40 weeks | New Ti-specific coupon testing not
captured; fatigue S-N data for new for

ging geometry required | 4–8 weeks (if not already initiated) |
| For
ging Die Design and Fabrication | Not currently scheduled | New die required
for revised hub geometry; Ti forging dies cost 2–3× aluminum

| 16–24 weeks; new activity |
| First Article Forging Production and
NDI | Not currently scheduled | After die qualification; includes FPI
per AMS 2647 and ultrasonic per A

MS 2632 | 12–18 weeks; new activity |
| Metal Fabrication Qualification (WBS 4.4.2) | 28
weeks (Jan–Aug 2028) | Full titanium process qualification required
; separate from aluminum qualification | +6–10 weeks; threatens

DTA-1 delivery |
| Supplier Qualification (WBS 4.5.1) | 24 weeks |
May require new or re-qualified titanium for

ging supplier | Concurrent with above; potentially gating |
| Hub Component
Fatigue Test | Not currently standalone | Full fatigue test program to
2× design life; new critical path activity | 24–52 weeks; program

-defining |
| DTA-1 Build (if already begun with
aluminum hub) | — | Non-representative test article if
material change not locked pre-fabrication | Potential full article

replacement |
| Hub Static Strength Test | Included in DTA-1 program | New

test article required for titanium design | 8–12 weeks incremental |
| FAA/
DER Re-coordination | Ongoing | New material
on primary dynamic structure requires revised compliance submission

| 4–12 weeks; intersects critical RISK-S-002 |
| F
TA-1 First Flight | PM 54 (Dec-2029) | Directly threatened if
hub qualification is not complete | 4–9 months at

risk |
| Type Certification | PM 84 | Hub re-qualification must close

before TC | 6–12 months at risk if late |

**Overall
Critical Path Assessment:** If the change is implemented after CD
R (PM 42) without immediate concurrent action, the worst-case schedule impact is **
6–9 months of critical path slip**, directly threatening First
Flight (Dec-2029) and cascading to Type Certification (PM 84) and PA-1 delivery
(Apr-2033). Even under the best-case scenario — change locked before
CDR with immediate material qualification initiation — a schedule pressure
of **3–5 months** must be actively managed against the existing

zero-float baseline.

### 3.3 Decision Window — Critical Timing

### 3.5 Block

-Change Alternative Schedule Posture

If the change cannot be locked before CDR, the program
should formally adopt a **block-change strategy at L

)**. Under this posture:

- LRIP-1 aircraft are built
to the current hub configuration (which meets baseline

requirements), preserving the First Flight and initial qualification schedule.
- The
redesigned titanium hub qualification campaign runs in parallel as a discrete
sub-program with its own schedule and budget, isolated from the primary certification

critical path.
- The redesigned hub enters production at LRIP-2 with full

qualification complete and zero disruption to the primary Type Certification timeline.
- This
posture is the preferred risk-managed alternative if the Go decision is not

made within the 56-day window defined above.

---

## 4. Change Management Plan

###

4.1 Change Classification and Governance

The proposed replacement of the main rotor hub is formally classified as a **Class I High-Impact
Engineering Change** under the program's change classification framework. This classification is established by the following criteria, all

of which are met:

- **Cost threshold exceeded:** Total program cost impact
estimated at $88M–$105M (central estimate ~

$94M), far exceeding the $500,000 Class I threshold.
- **New tool
ing fabrication required:** New Ti-6Al-4V forging dies, machining fixtures

, and inspection tooling are required.
- **Supplier qualification required:** New or re-qualification of the titanium forging and machining

supply chain is required.
- **Multi-variant impact:** The hub is a Green Aircraft common component affecting all three variants simultaneously.
- **Safety
-critical component:** The hub is an explicitly named Safety-Critical Items List (

SCIL) item under SRD-HAZ-006.
- **Schedule impact exce
eds 8 weeks:** Critical path impact estimated at 3–9 months depending

on timing.

### 4.2 Change Control Board Composition and Responsibilities

The CC

B for this change shall include, at minimum, the following members with the following roles:

| Member | Role

| Authority |
|---|---|---|
| Program Manager (Chair) | Decision authority for Class I changes | Approve, reject

, or defer with conditions |
| Chief Engineer | Technical disposition and design authority | Technical

go/no-go recommendation |
| VP Operations | Manufacturing and supply chain feasibility | Resource commitment authority

|
| VP Finance / Program Controller | Cost and margin disposition | Management reserve authorization

|
| Program Executive Officer | Common configuration waiver co-authority | Required sign

atory for Green Aircraft change |
| Supply Chain Director | Procurement and supplier commitment | Sole

-source and advance payment authorization |
| Manufacturing Engineering Manager | Process qualification

and tooling feasibility | Manufacturing readiness disposition |
| Government Program Representative (

if applicable) | Class I change concurrence | Customer equitable adjustment position |

### 4.3

ECP Content Requirements

The Engineering Change Proposal submitted to the CCB must contain the following elements
, all of which must be complete before the CCB convenes for

disposition:

1. **BOM Discrepancy Resolution Memorandum** — signed by Chief Engineer and Controller, confir
ming whether the current cost baseline reflects aluminum or titanium hub pricing and the disposition of the configuration

management discrepancy.

2. **Technical Trade Study** — 30-day structural
analysis comparing (a) redesigned titanium hub, (b) current aluminum hub with fatigue enhancement
(shot peening, cold working, surface treatment), and (c) current B
OM titanium hub as-is. Must include quantified weight delta
, fatigue life improvement, and cost-per-fatigue-life-hour metric for each option

.

3. **Cross-Variant Impact Assessment** — per PRB Section
5.1 waiver requirements, addressing cost, schedule, and logistics footprint for MAAP-1F

, MAAP-1I, MAAP-1C, and prospective MAAP-1N.

4. **Class I vs. Class II Classification
Determination** — formal assessment of whether the fatigue life improvement addresses a government-identified
deficiency (Class I, customer-fundable) or is an internally initiated
improvement (Class II, internal absorption). This determination directly controls whether
the $94M central cost estimate is a contract modification candidate

or a management reserve draw.

5. **Should-Cost Estimate** — detailed bottom-up cost estimate for N
RE (forging die, tooling, qualification testing
, engineering redesign) and recurring unit cost delta, with

confidence bounds and basis of estimate.

6. **Schedule Impact Analysis** — I
MS-referenced impact assessment identifying critical path effects, float consumption, and milestone
dates at risk, with and without the block-change alternative.
7. **Risk Assessment** — all new and elevated risks identified in this PMP Update, with initial

likelihood and consequence scores.

8. **Mitigation Portfolio** — quant

ified mitigation options as specified in Section 4.4 below.

### 4.4 Mitigation Portfolio

The following mi
tigations must be evaluated and their cost and schedule impacts quantified before CC

B disposition:

**Mitigation 1 — Class I Customer Funding Pursuit (Priority

: Immediate)**
Engage the customer to determine whether the fatigue life improvement addresses a government-
identified specification deficiency. If the original specification underspecified fatigue life and the customer agrees,
the change becomes a Class I Engineering Change with potential for equitable adjustment. Even
50% customer cost recovery would reduce internal exposure from
$94M to approximately $47M — a program-preserving outcome on an FF
P contract. The Program Manager and Contracts officer must assess
the customer relationship and contractual basis for this action before the

CCB convenes.

**Mitigation 2 — Aluminum Fatigue Enhancement Alternative (Priority: Immediate)**
Commission
a 30-day structural trade study comparing the titanium redes
ign against an aluminum hub with fatigue life enhancement techniques (shot peening, cold working of fastener
holes, hard anodize, surface treatment). If the aluminum alternative
can achieve 80% or more of the fatigue life improvement at
20% or less of the cost, this is the preferred path. The NRE for an
aluminum fatigue enhancement program is estimated at less than $5M versus the $26
M central NRE estimate for titanium — a compelling cost-risk

tradeoff that must be formally evaluated before committing to titanium.

**Mitigation 3

— Leverage Existing Titanium Capability (Priority: If Change Approved)**
The program already produces
titanium components (engine mounts per BOM, blade leading edge eros
ion strips). Leveraging existing NADCAP-certified titanium machining processes and qualified
suppliers for the hub program can reduce NRE by an
estimated $1.5M–$3M relative to treating titanium hub

machining as an entirely new process establishment.

**Mitigation 4 — Sole-Source Supplier with Multi

-Year Fixed-Price Commitment (Priority: If Change Approved)**
Negotiate a multi-year fixed-price for
ging agreement with a single qualified titanium forging supplier before the hub design is finalized,
capturing pricing before the supplier recognizes their leverage. This approach
, analogous to the GE engine strategy referenced in the
financial analysis, can reduce material cost growth risk over

the program life by an estimated $15M–$25M versus spot market procurement.

**Mitigation

5 — Combined Qualification Testing (Priority: If Change Approved)**
Pursue combination of hub
qualification testing with the existing DTA-1 structural test program rather
than standing up a separate qualification article, avoiding an estimated $4
M–$7M in incremental test article and test stand cost
. This requires schedule alignment and must be assessed against D

TA-1 structural test objectives.

**Mitigation 6 — R&D Tax Credit Capture (Priority: If Change Approved)**
All qualifying
Ti hub development costs — engineering redesign labor
, coupon testing, component qualification — are eligible for the R&D tax credit under IRC
§41. Estimated tax benefit: $2M–$4M.
The Controller must update the tax credit analysis to include hub redesign NRE.
**Total Mitigation Potential: $22.5M–$39M**
— meaningful but insufficient to offset the central $94M program cost impact
without customer funding. The CCB must understand this explicitly.

### 4.5 Go/No-Go Decision Framework

| Condition | CCB Recommendation |

|---|---|
| BOM already Ti, design already mature
and qualified | No action required — close change request

; issue CM discrepancy notice |

## 7. Procurement / Supply Chain Management

Plan

### 7.1 Make/Buy Determination

The make/buy determination
for the redesigned titanium hub is confirmed as **BU
Y**, consistent with the existing make/buy strategy for the rotor system. The rat
ionale is unchanged and reinforced by the titanium material change:
- Ti-6Al-4V hub forgings for rotorcraft applications require closed-die forging capability
, specialized heat treatment (isothermal or hot-
die forging), and NDI infrastructure (FPI per AMS 2647,
ultrasonic per AMS 2632) that Eurus does not possess and should

not invest in at current program volumes.
- The qualification regime
for a safety-critical primary rotating structure requires that manufacturing process qualification be managed
by a supplier with NADCAP certification for titan
ium machining and forging — a certification level that Eurus's internal

machining capability does not currently hold for this component class.
- Design authority
must remain in-house. Regardless of the B
UY manufacturing decision, Eurus retains ownership of fatigue analysis

, stress substantiation, and certification compliance data as Eurus intellectual property.

One
nuance warrants feasibility assessment: if the hub redesign involves significant
post-forging machining of features that are within Eurus's current
5-axis CNC capability envelope, a **forge-out
/ machine-in-house hybrid approach** may reduce
lead time risk during LRIP certification iterations. This should
be evaluated in the 30-day technical trade study but should not delay the primary

BUY decision.

The
titanium forging supply chain for this component class operates on significantly

longer lead times than the aluminum equivalent:

Design) | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forging die design and

fabrication | 16–26 weeks | 26–52 weeks | +10–26 weeks |
| First

article forging production | 8–12 weeks | 8–12 weeks | Neutral |
| Mach
ining and NDI (FPI + ultrasonic) | 3–5 weeks | 6–9 weeks | +3–4

weeks |
| Static strength testing | 6–8 weeks | 8–12 weeks | +2–4 weeks |
| Fat
igue test program | 12–24 weeks | 24–52 weeks | +12–28

weeks |
| **Total to first flight-qualified hub** | **45–75

weeks** | **72–137 weeks** | **+27–62 weeks** |

The current titanium forging market
has extended lead times from historical 26–32 weeks to 36
–48 weeks for large, complex Ti-6Al-4V forgings, following the exclus
ion of VSMPO-AVISMA from Western supply chains in 2022. This market reality
is not reflected in the current BOM or procurement

plan and must be incorporated into the revised supply chain strategy.

**
Critical Implication:** Under the block-change scenario (LRIP-1

igned titanium hub), the procurement team must place forging orders for the redesigned hub no later than Q

### 7.4 Critical Item List Update

The following additions and

modifications to the Critical Item List (CIL) are required immediately:

- **New CIL Entry
— Ti-6Al-4V Hub Forgings (AF-MAAP1-RS-1001
-01):** Meets all CIL criteria: Long Lead Time (36
–52 weeks), Single/Dual Source risk (constrained domestic supplier base
), High Technical Risk (new forging geometry, first article
required), and Safety-Critical classification (SCIL per S

RD-HAZ-006).
- **New CIL Entry — Ti-6Al-4V
Billet Feedstock:** Long lead time, constrained domestic supply

, safety-critical end use.
- **Escalate existing
hub entry** on CIL ranking to reflect material

change and re-qualification burden.

### 7.5 Production Ramp Supply Chain Risks

The production
ramp schedule targets 2 aircraft per month at Full Rate Production, ultimately
scaling to 18 aircraft per year at peak FRP capacity. Each aircraft requires two hubs.
At FRP, this requires 4 flight-qualified titanium hub
forgings per month (assuming zero spares production). Against a 36–
48 week forging lead time, the supply chain must carry a minimum **9–
12 month forging inventory buffer** at FRP to prevent production st
arvation. The current manufacturing ramp plan does not include this buffer, and the cash flow implications of carrying
$485K+ forgings in 9

–12 month inventory must be assessed.

The 87% learning curve assumption
for the rotor system supply chain (or 85% per the
program-level plan) must be revised to **90–
92% for the hub machining element specifically
**, as titanium hub machining constitutes a new process restart. This revision, combined with higher
raw material costs, will delay the cost reduction trajectory toward

the FRP-2 unit cost target and must be reflected in the revised EAC.

---

## 8. Quality

Management Plan

### 8.1 Safety-Critical Item Obligations

The main ro
tor hub is an explicitly named Safety-Critical Items List (SCIL) item
under SRD-HAZ-006. A material change to a SCIL item triggers
the following mandatory quality obligations, all of which restart from

zero for the new design:

- New raw material certifications (Ti-6Al-4V mill cert
ifications per AMS 4928 or successor specification, per

forging lot).
- New serialized component history records from

first article production.
- Full enhanced receiving inspection protocol rewritten
for titanium — eddy current inspection parameters differ from aluminum; fluor
escent penetrant inspection (FPI) per AMS 2647 and ultrasonic inspection per AMS 2632 are both

required for titanium forgings of this criticality class.
- "No
equivalent substitution" clause enforcement — the aluminum hub, if previously
qualified, cannot be used interchangeably with the titanium hub without a full
engineering analysis. The two configurations must be treated as distinct part numbers

with separate quality histories throughout any transition period.

### 8.2 Qualification

Test Program

The qualification test program for the redesigned titanium hub constitutes the single largest schedule
and cost risk associated with this change. The following qualification activities
are required and must be reflected in an updated Test and Evaluation Master Plan

(TEMP):

**Building-Block Test Program:**

|

Test Level | Test Activity | Duration | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coupon | Material characterization; S-N
curve development; B-basis allowables for
Ti-6Al-4V AMS 4928 in hub geometry | 8–16 weeks | Must
use same forging process and heat treatment as production intent

|
| Element | Joint and lug fatigue specimens at
representative stress concentrations | 12–20 weeks | Notch sensitivity, fretting at blade

retention interfaces |
| Component | Hub body under representative load spectrum
; bearing retention under combined loads | 24–40 weeks (

accelerated) | Critical path driver |
| Full Scale | Hub
assembly fatigue test to 2× design life (
40,000 flight hour equivalent per SRD-ST
R-002) | 52–104 weeks | Full spectrum loading at rot

orcraft N/rev harmonics |
| Static Strength | Limit and ultimate
load test (5.25g pull
-up, ±3.0g lateral,
24,000 lb hook loads per Key Specs

) | 8–12 weeks | New test article required for Ti design |
| Dynamic
/ Vibration | Rotor system ground resonance; vib
ration survey (≤0.50 in/sec RMS per TEMP
7.3.7); rotor track
and balance (±0.5 in tip tolerance per Key Specs
6.3) | 8–16 weeks | New hub mass/

stiffness properties may alter rotor tuning |
| Rotor Oversp
eed (Ground) | Governor failure simulation per SRD-
ROTOR-003; structural margin verification at 288 RPM /
107% transient limit | 4–8 weeks | Required for revised hub st

iffness |
| Environmental | Corrosion (salt fog, thermal cycling per MIL-STD-810
G); galvanic compatibility | 12–16 weeks | Validate surface treatment

; verify isolation at Al interface components |
| Lightning Strike
| Indirect effects on hub sensors and instrumentation per
DO-160 | 4–6 weeks | Titanium conduct

ivity differs from aluminum |
| **Total Qualification Campaign** | | **132–238
weeks from test article delivery** | Overlap of concurrent
activities reduces effective program duration; fatigue test is the critical path item

|

The **full-scale fatigue test to 2× design life is the
program-defining qualification activity**. A 40,000 flight
-hour equivalent test at rotorcraft load spectra, even with an
accelerated schedule, requires 52–104 weeks of continuous operation. This test cannot be compressed without
risk to airworthiness substantiation. If this
test is initiated at PM 36 (concurrent with other qualification activities), it compl
etes between PM 49 and PM 62 — straddling the First Flight milestone (PM 54
) and the Structural Qualification Review (PM 54). Program management must determine
whether first flight can proceed on an approved partial

qualification basis or must await test completion.

### 8.3

First Article Inspection Requirements

First Article Inspection (FAI) per AS
9102 must be conducted on the first production titanium hub for
ging from the production-intent supply chain (not on
prototype or test article forgings from a different process). The FAI scope

includes:

- 100% dimensional verification against the revised hub drawing
- Material

certification verification (AMS 4928 compliance, lot traceability)
- Process

certification verification (NADCAP forging and machining process)
- NDI results review (FPI and

ultrasonic records)
- Surface finish verification at bearing seats, mast interface

, and lug bores
- Serialization and SCIL record establishment

FA
I completion is a prerequisite to production LRIP rate
authorization and must be a named milestone in the revised I

MS.

### 8.4 Inspection Protocol Updates

The following inspection-related quality

plan updates are required:

**Manufacturing Inspection:**
- Titanium machining introduces unique quality
escape modes not present in aluminum machining: hard alpha
inclusions (subsurface defects not visually detectable), impro
per heat treatment (impacts fatigue life non-linearly), and titanium fire risk
from chip accumulation. Work instructions must address all

three.
- Dedicated titanium chip management, segregated waste streams, and updated
Job Hazard Analysis (JHA) are required under the program's ISO 45

001 EHS objectives.
- Carbide cutting tool condition monitoring and
mandatory replacement intervals (titanium's low thermal conductivity concentrates heat
at the cutting edge, causing rapid and non-obvious tool degradation) must be incorporated

into process control documents.

**In-Service Inspection:**
- Titanium hub inspection intervals must be formally
derived from the new crack growth analysis and fatigue substantiation, not

assumed from the aluminum design's existing intervals.
- The maintenance
task hazard table (SRD-GND-005) must be updated to address
: titanium swarf handling (Class D combust
ible metal — more hazardous than aluminum), anti-seize compound application
at titanium fastener interfaces (galling risk is higher

than aluminum), and updated PPE requirements for hub maintenance at height.
- ND
I inspection techniques for in-service titanium hub inspection must be qualified: eddy current applicability
(limited by titanium's low electrical conductivity), fluorescent penetrant (applicable for surface
-breaking cracks), and phased-array ultrasonic (applicable for subs
urface damage tolerance inspection). The current maintenance plan
may specify aluminum-appropriate techniques that are non-conservative

for titanium.

### 8.5 Process Capability and MRL Requirements

The program's Manufacturing
Readiness Level (MRL) targets MRL 9 by
LRIP-3, which requires demonstrated process stability with Cp
k ≥ 1.33 on critical characteristics. Titanium hub machining processes are new
to this facility and will not have accumulated the
process history necessary to demonstrate Cpk ≥ 1.33 on hub
critical characteristics (bearing bore diameter, lug hole locations
, mast taper dimensions) by LRIP-3 without a dedicated
process development and validation effort beginning no later than the Manufacturing Development

Phase (Dec 2027).

This requirement must be explicitly added to the LRIP-3 gate
criteria, with a named responsible engineer and a process capability demonstration

plan incorporated into the Quality Management Plan.

### 8.6 Hazard Analysis Updates

The following Hazard Analysis Report

(HAR) updates are required upon approval of the titanium hub change:

- **HA
Z-016 (Rotor Overspeed — Catastrophic/
Remote):** Update to document improved structural margins from titan
ium fatigue life. Revalidate overspeed structural limits
with titanium hub stiffness and mass properties. If margins improve

sufficiently, assess whether residual risk classification can be reduced.
- **HAZ-025
(Maintenance Error):** Update maintenance task hazard table to reflect titanium-specific maintenance risks (swarf,
anti-seize, torque procedures, specialized NDI tooling).
- **FTA Update (Rotor Speed Governing,
SRD-HAZ-005 Item 10):** Update to reflect titanium hub failure
modes and revised structural margins as a barrier against HAZ-016.

---

## 9. Staffing & Resource Management

### 9.1 Engineering Staffing Impact

The proposed
change consumes significant engineering capacity from a workforce already operating
at the program's documented 350-person peak engineering staff

level. The change introduces the following incremental engineering labor demands:

| Engineering
Function | Estimated Incremental Effort | Duration | FTE Equivalent

|
|---|---|---|---|
| Structural Analysis (hub redesign, FE
A, fatigue analysis, DTA correlation) | 15,000–
25,000 hours | 18–24 months | 4–7 FT

Es sustained |
| Materials Engineering (Ti qualification, coupon test oversight, allow
ables development) | 3,000–6,000 hours | 12–18 months | 1–2 FTEs sustained |
| Rotor Dynamics (hub mass/stiffness update, blade frequency
recheck, ground resonance) | 4,000–8,000 hours | 6–12 months | 2–3 FTEs sustained

|
| Manufacturing Engineering (tooling design, Ti process qualification, work
instruction rewrite) | 5,000–10,000 hours | 12–18 months | 2–3 FTEs sustained |
| Quality and Inspection Engineering (NDI procedure development, FAI planning, HAR updates) | 2,000–4,000

hours | 12 months | 1–2 FTEs sustained |
| Certification / DER
Coordination (compliance matrix update, FAA ACO coordination) | 2

,000–5,000 hours | 12–24 months | 1–2 FTEs sustained |
| Systems Engineering (
RVTM update, verification method changes, PRB waiver package

) | 1,000–2,000 hours | 3–6 months | 0.5–1 FTE |
| **Total Incremental Engineering

FTE-equivalent** |

This represents an **11.5–20 FTE sustained
demand over 18–24 months** against a workforce baseline that
has no documented excess capacity. The incremental demand must be met through one or

more of the following:

- **Option 1 — Re-priorit
ization:** De-scope or defer lower-priority engineering activities to create internal
capacity. This requires formal identification of activities to defer

and Chief Engineer approval.
- **Option 2 — Targeted Hiring:** Recruit specialists in titanium structural
analysis, titanium machining process engineering, and rotorcraft fatigue testing.
Given RISK-M-004's documentation of specialized labor as
an existing program risk, this option faces a competitive

hiring environment and premium wage costs.
- **Option 3 — Sub
contract Engineering Support:** Engage a qualified aerospace engineering services
firm for specific deliverables (e.g., Ti coupon test management
, FEA correlation, compliance documentation). This introduces a new subcontract

management workload but avoids headcount growth.
- **Option 4 — Supplier
Engineering Engagement:** Direct the hub supplier (Bell or
equivalent) to perform hub-specific redesign engineering under the amended
CPFF contract, with Eurus retaining design review and approval

authority.

The Program Manager must select and authorize the staffing approach within
30 days of CCB approval. The approach must be documented in a formal Resource Loading Update to the I

MS.

### 9.2 Hub Qualification Lead — Dedicated Role

Given
the criticality of the hub qualification test program to the First Flight milestone and the program's zero-float
schedule, a **dedicated Hub Qualification Lead** must be established as a named position with the

following responsibilities:

- Weekly reporting to the Chief Engineer on qualification test progress
- Authority to escal
ate schedule or technical issues to the Program Director between PMR

cycles
- Coordination of all hub-related activities
across structures, materials, manufacturing, supply chain, and certification functions
- Ownership of the go/no-go gate assessment at PM [CD

R+4 months]

This role should be filled by a senior
structures engineer with titanium forging and rotorcraft fatigue testing experience. If
this profile is not available internally, external recruitment or technical consultant

engagement is required before ECP approval.

### 9.3 Manufacturing Workforce — Titanium Machining Specialists
The program's existing manufacturing workforce was selected and trained for aluminum and composite

fabrication. Titanium machining of safety-critical rotating components requires:

- **Specialized Mac
hinists:** Operators with titanium-specific cutting experience (low feed rates, high-pressure
coolant discipline, tool condition recognition). Existing
workforce may require 3–6 months of titanium machining training before

being qualified on flight-hardware production.
- **NDI Specialists —
FPI and Ultrasonic:** Titanium inspection per AMS 2647 (FPI) and AMS 2632
(ultrasonic) requires inspectors qualified to ASNT Level
II or III with titanium-specific experience. FPI and ultrasonic inspection parameters for titanium differ
from aluminum; existing inspector qualifications may not transfer without re-certification

.
- **Fire Safety Training:** Titanium chip handling requires Class D fire safety training not currently
required in the aluminum fabrication environment. All personnel in

the titanium machining area must complete this training before production.

These workforce requirements must be incorporated into the training and
qualification plan, with estimated training costs of $150K–$300K added to program

NRE.

### 9.4 Supplier Resource Commitments

The hub supplier
(Bell or equivalent) must demonstrate adequate engineering and manufacturing resource commitment to the hub redesign scope before

the contract amendment is executed. The program should:

- Require a
dedicated engineering team commitment, with named discipline leads and

minimum staffing levels, as a contract term.
- Include
milestone-based payments that incentivize resource deployment (not just deliverable completion), given

the critical-path nature of the hub qualification program.
- Conduct quarterly supplier
resource reviews as part of the existing supplier management cadence, with right-to

-audit provisions for engineering and manufacturing capacity.

### 9.5 Test Infrastructure

The qualification test program requires the

following test infrastructure resources that must be confirmed available before ECP approval:

- **Component Fatigue
Rig:** A dedicated hub assembly fatigue test rig capable of applying representative rotorcraft load
spectra (combined centrifugal, flap bending, lead-lag b
ending, and torsion loads) to the titanium hub. If this rig is not currently in the
program's 12 major test stand inventory, it must be procured or contracted.
Estimated cost: $2M–$5M; lead time: 12–18 months

.
- **Whirl Tower Availability:** The whirl tower test schedule
must be assessed for availability to support titanium hub integration testing without confl
icting with other qualification activities. If whirl tower time is fully committed to existing qualification activities, hub

integration testing may require either schedule extension or external test facility engagement.
- **F
PI Inspection Line:** A titanium-qualified FPI inspection line per AMS 2647 must be available,
either at the hub supplier or at an external inspection facility. Lead time for
new FPI line qualification: 16–24 weeks.

---

## 10. Overall Assessment & Recommended Path Forward

### 10.1 Integrated

Assessment Summary

The proposed replacement of the aluminum main rotor hub with a titanium design is technically motivated by
a genuine and meaningful fatigue life objective for the highest
-cycle component on the aircraft. Ti-6Al-4V's superior fatigue end
urance limit — approximately 60 ksi versus aluminum
's 22 ksi at 10⁷ cycles — makes it the correct material choice for a
rotor hub that must demonstrate a safe-life limit exce
eding the 5,000 flight-hour blade service life requirement and

contribute to the program's fleet availability and maintainability objectives.

However
, the analysis across all nine program documents examined reveals a converg
ent and unambiguous conclusion: **the change as currently
proposed cannot be approved as a routine engineering change, and cannot be absorbed
as an internally funded Class II change under current

program financial conditions.** The reasons are as follows:

**On configuration:** The B
OM already specifies a titanium hub, and this discrepancy must be resolved before any other
action. Proceeding without resolving this creates compounding configuration management

risk.

**On cost:** The central program cost impact of approximately $52M (
rising to $94M under adverse interpretation) against a $74
M management reserve, on a Firm Fixed Price contract with
no cost recovery mechanism, is a program-threatening financial event. The program
's own change control policy prohibits Class II changes that are
not margin-neutral or positive. This change fails

that threshold decisively under every scenario analyzed.

**On schedule:** The
IMS has zero float. The hub qualification program —
particularly the full-scale fatigue test to 2× design life — is a multi-year activity that does
not fit within the existing zero-float critical path without a schedule impact ranging
from 3 months (best case, pre
-CDR action) to 9 months (worst case, post-CDR action). A 6-month First Flight delay
alone costs $62M and −147 basis points of margin

, independent of the direct hub change costs.

**On manufacturing:**
Titanium machining is not equivalent to aluminum machining. The production rate targets (2 aircraft/month at
FRP), the 85% learning curve assumptions, the tooling investment basis
, and the supply chain lead time assumptions in the current manufacturing r
amp plan are all invalid for a titanium hub without significant recalibration. The hub
machining cell, with two hubs per aircraft and titanium cycle
times 5–10× longer than aluminum, is a credible FRP production rate

bottleneck that has not been analyzed.

**On risk:** This change introduces five Critical
-scored risks (scores of 16–20) and three
High-scored risks that are not currently in the risk register. Combined with the elevation of two
existing Critical risks (RISK-S-001 and RISK-S-002), the aggregate
risk exposure from this single change rivals the program's entire documented risk portfolio

in magnitude.

**On the benefit side:** The fatigue life improvement
is real, operationally meaningful, and aligned with the program's fleet availability objectives. Titanium's
inherent corrosion resistance is a genuine advantage, particularly for the future
naval variant. The change, if properly executed on the right
timeline with adequate resources, would produce a superior product. The question
is not whether titanium is the right answer — it may well be — but whether the current
program condition (zero-float schedule, FFP contract, 9.
9% margin target, and a management reserve that
the change would substantially consume) is the right time and mechanism

to make this change.

### 10.2 Recommended Decision Framework

The CCB must make its decision within the
56-day decision window defined in Section 3.3. The following

path forward is recommended, presented as a sequenced decision tree:

**Step 1 — Mandatory Prerequis
ite (Days 0–10): Resolve BOM Discrepancy**
Convene the emergency engineering/finance review. Determine
definitively whether the cost baseline reflects aluminum or titanium hub pricing. If the
design is already titanium and the qualification program is already funded and
underway, close the change request with a configuration management
corrective action notice. This is the only scenario that does not require the full decision

framework below.

**Step 2 — Pursue Class I Classification (Days 0

–30, concurrent with Step 1)**
Engage the customer immediately to determine
whether the fatigue life improvement addresses a government-identified specification def
iciency. Prepare a technical case demonstrating that the aluminum hub (if that
was the original design intent) does not meet the fatigue life implied by the program's KPPs for
fleet availability, inspection interval, and mission readiness rate. Even
partial Class I classification (50% customer funding) transforms this

from a program-threatening event to a manageable one.

**Step 3 — Execute the

Technical Trade Study (Days 0–30, concurrent)**
Commission the
30-day structural trade study comparing: (a) redesigned titanium hub, (b) aluminum hub with fat
igue enhancement, and (c) current BOM design as-is. The trade study must produce
quantified weight delta, fatigue life improvement, cost-per
-fatigue-life-hour, and schedule impact for each option. This is the analytical

basis for the CCB's material decision and cannot be bypassed.

**Step 4

— CCB Disposition (Day 56)**
Based on Steps 1–

3, the CCB selects one of four paths:

*Path A — Close the Change (if
BOM is already Ti and design is qualified):*
Issue CM corrective action; no further program impact

.

*Path B — Proceed with Class I Customer Funding (if ≥50% customer cost recovery
secured):* Approve ECP; initiate titanium material qualification
immediately; manage residual ~$26–47
M internally through mitigation portfolio activation. This is the preferred outcome

if achievable.

*Path C — Block-Change at LRIP-2
with Parallel Qualification Campaign (if no customer funding available but decision is made before
CDR):* Approve titanium hub change with LRIP-2 incorporation point; authorize management
reserve draw for NRE ($21M central estimate
); run qualification campaign on parallel track; protect LRIP-1 and First
Flight schedule. This is the preferred risk-managed path if Class I classification fails

.

*Path D — Reject and Implement Aluminum Fatigue Enhancement (if trade study shows
aluminum alternative achieves ≥80% of fatigue life benefit):* Reject titanium redes
ign as Class II (fails margin-neutral threshold); implement aluminum hub
with shot peening, cold working, and surface treatment at estimated
NRE of <$5M. This is the correct decision if the trade study supports it
, and should not be dismissed as a lesser outcome —
a properly executed aluminum fatigue enhancement program may be the optimal program

-level decision.

### 10.3 Non-Negotiable Immediate Actions

Regardless of the CCB disposition, the following actions

must be initiated immediately and are not contingent on the change decision:

1
. **Freeze aluminum hub design and assign ECP number** — no
further design evolution on any hub configuration without CC

B authorization.

2. **Conduct emergency BOM discrepancy review** — engineering and finance,

completed within 10 business days, signed by Chief Engineer and Controller.

3. **Initiate titanium for
ging market survey** — Supply Chain Director, completed within 30 days, regardless of change decision (
the market intelligence has program-wide value for the existing B

OM if it already specifies titanium).

4. **Commission the 30-day technical trade study** — Chief

Engineer, initiated immediately, completed within 30 days.

5. **Register all new
and elevated risks in the Risk Register** — Risk Manager, within five business days of
this PMP Update's issuance, with owners

and initial mitigation actions assigned.

6. **Notify FAA ACO of
potential major design change** — Certification Lead, within 30 days, to
preserve FAA coordination relationship and avoid surprise at compliance submission

.

7. **Update the IMS to reflect zero-float criticality of hub decision timeline
** — Schedule Manager, within 10 business days, adding the 56-day decision window as

a program milestone.

### 10.4 Program Leadership Guidance

The central message
for program leadership is this: the titanium main rotor hub is the right long
-term answer for this aircraft. The operational benefits — fatigue life,
corrosion resistance, fleet availability, and lifecycle cost — are real and will distinguish the MA
AP-1 in competition for sustainment and export
business. The question is not whether to get there, but when and

how to get there without destroying the financial and schedule foundation of the program in the process.

The program should
pursue every available avenue to make this a customer-funded
Class I change, because the technical case for it — properly
framed around the government's interest in fleet availability and KPP
compliance — is strong. If that avenue closes, the L
RIP-2 block-change approach preserves program integrity while delivering the fatigue life improvement before
the fleet reaches meaningful accumulated hours. What the program cannot
afford, and what the CCB must prevent, is an ad
-hoc implementation of this change without the governance, financial authorization, qualification plan

, and supply chain strategy that a change of this magnitude demands.

The path
forward is clear. The decision window is 56 days. The CCB must act.

---

*End of Project Management Plan Update — MAAP-1*
*

Document Status: Draft for CCB Review*
*Next Review: CCB Session [

Date TBD within 56-day decision window]*

: Program Manager, MAAP-1 AetherForge*
*Approval
Required: Program Director, Chief Engineer, VP Finance, Program Executive

# Project Management Plan Update — MAAP-1

**Document Control**
Program: AetherForge MAAP-1 Tandem-Rotor Heavy-Lift Autonomous Helicopter
Issuing Organization: Eurus Systems Program Management Office
Document Type: Program Management Plan (PMP) Update — Engineering Change Assessment
Change Subject: Proposed Replacement of Aluminum Main Rotor Hub with Titanium Design
Contract Type: Firm Fixed Price (FFP)
Total Contract Value: $4,128M
Update Classification: Class I Engineering Change — Full CCB Review Required
Prepared By: Program Manager, with inputs from Cost & Margin, Manufacturing, Supply Chain, Change Impact, and Risk & Schedule functional leads
Distribution: Program Director, Chief Engineer, VP Operations, VP Finance, Program Executive Officer, Change Control Board Members

---

## 1. Introduction

### 1.1 Purpose of This Update

This Project Management Plan (PMP) Update formally documents the programmatic response to the proposed engineering change to replace the aluminum main rotor hub with a titanium design on the MAAP-1 AetherForge program. The update captures the integrated impact of this proposed change across all ten program management domains, synthesizes findings from specialized functional analyses conducted across the full program document baseline, and establishes the authoritative management framework within which the Change Control Board (CCB) will evaluate, approve, conditionally approve, or reject the change.

This document supplements and partially supersedes relevant sections of the baseline PMP. All sections herein reflect the state of analysis as of the assessment date and shall be updated upon CCB disposition of the Engineering Change Proposal (ECP).

### 1.2 Background and Change Rationale

The proposed change seeks to replace the main rotor hub material from aluminum (assumed baseline for change proposal purposes) with Ti-6Al-4V, driven by a fatigue life improvement objective. The primary technical motivation is sound: Ti-6Al-4V exhibits a genuine fatigue endurance limit — approximately 60 ksi at 10⁷ cycles compared to aluminum 7075-T7351's effective fatigue limit of approximately 22 ksi — making titanium substantially superior for a component that experiences continuous, high-cycle loading at N/rev and higher harmonics throughout every flight hour. The rotor hub is the highest-cycle fatigue component on the aircraft, and improved fatigue life translates directly into extended inspection intervals, higher fleet availability, and reduced lifecycle cost.

However, a critical preliminary finding fundamentally reframes the analysis: the current program Bill of Materials (BOM, AF-MAAP1-RS-1001-00) already specifies the Main Rotor Hub Assembly as Ti-6Al-4V Forging, AMS 4928 at $485,000 per unit with a Long Lead (LL) designation. The Blade Grip Assembly (AF-MAAP1-RS-1003-00, five units per aircraft) is similarly specified as Ti-6Al-4V at $95,000 per set. This finding generates three possible interpretations, each with distinct programmatic consequences:

**Interpretation A — Configuration Management Discrepancy:** The BOM has been updated to reflect a titanium hub that has not been formally authorized through the CCB process. The BOM is ahead of the change control system, constituting a configuration management failure requiring immediate corrective action.

**Interpretation B — Redesign Within Material Baseline:** The current titanium hub design, while correctly specified as Ti-6Al-4V, does not achieve the fatigue life objectives required by the program's Key Performance Parameters (KPPs). The change proposes a geometric redesign of the hub forging to exploit titanium's material properties more fully — a new dash number, new forging envelope, revised lug geometry — while retaining the same alloy.

**Interpretation C — Legacy Aluminum Design in Active Drawing Tree:** The BOM reflects a forward-priced optimistic baseline, but the actual current design drawings still reference an aluminum hub that has not been formally superseded. The cost baseline may therefore not reflect actual procurement intent.

**This PMP Update proceeds on the assumption that Interpretation B or C represents the operative scenario**, as these carry the most significant programmatic consequences. Interpretation A (BOM is current and the change is already accomplished) would substantially reduce the scope of action required; however, it would not eliminate the configuration management corrective actions documented herein. The CCB must resolve which interpretation applies before any design or procurement action proceeds. Until that determination is made, this document treats the proposed change as a live engineering change requiring full evaluation.

### 1.3 Governance Authority

The main rotor hub is explicitly designated a common Green Aircraft configuration item under Program Requirements Baseline (PRB) Section 5.1, which enumerates the rotor system, transmissions, drive train, and rotor blades as components shared identically across all three aircraft variants: MAAP-1F (firefighting), MAAP-1I (ISR), and MAAP-1C (cargo). As a consequence:

- Any deviation from common configuration status requires a formal waiver approved by the Chief Engineer and the Program Executive Officer.
- The impact assessment submitted in support of that waiver must explicitly address cost, schedule, and logistics footprint across all three variants.
- The change must be processed exclusively through the Program CCB, chaired by the Program Manager.
- No design authority action, procurement commitment, or supplier direction may be issued prior to CCB disposition.

The proposed change is classified as a **Class I Engineering Change** under the program's change classification framework: it involves new tooling fabrication, supplier qualification or re-qualification, multi-variant impacts, and cost impact estimated to exceed $500,000 by several orders of magnitude. Class I changes require Program Manager approval at minimum and, given the financial magnitude documented herein, require escalation to VP level and formal management reserve authorization.

### 1.4 Scope of This PMP Update

This update addresses the following program management domains:

- Project scope definition and boundary conditions for the change
- Milestone and schedule baseline impacts, including critical path analysis
- Change management process, governance, and decision authorities
- Cost management, including EAC revision, management reserve assessment, and FFP contract exposure
- Risk management, including new and elevated risks requiring immediate register action
- Procurement and supply chain management, including supplier qualification, lead time, and make/buy implications
- Quality management, including qualification testing, inspection, and airworthiness certification
- Staffing and resource management, including engineering capacity and specialized workforce requirements
- Overall assessment and recommended path forward for CCB decision

---

## 2. Project Scope

### 2.1 Baseline Scope Context

The MAAP-1 program scope encompasses the design, development, qualification, and production of a tandem-rotor heavy-lift autonomous helicopter across three variants (MAAP-1F, MAAP-1I, MAAP-1C) with a production baseline of 183 units under the current FFP contract. The program is structured around a common Green Aircraft platform — a shared structural and dynamic core — with variant-specific mission system overlays. The rotor system is the highest-value and highest-complexity element of the Green Aircraft, representing approximately $1.235M per aircraft in procurement cost for the main rotor assembly alone.

The program's integrated master schedule (IMS) operates on a 92-month baseline with zero float on the critical path. The rotor system development cycle is explicitly identified as a 54-month critical path item. The program is currently approaching CDR (PM 42), with Manufacturing Development (Phase 4) beginning December 2027, DTA-1 fabrication scheduled for July 2029, and First Flight targeted for December 2029.

### 2.2 Scope of the Proposed Change

The proposed change, as analyzed under Interpretation B (the operative scenario for this PMP Update), constitutes a redesigned titanium hub forging with improved fatigue geometry — a new forging envelope, revised hub lug geometry, modified bearing retention features, and updated structural cross-sections designed to exploit Ti-6Al-4V's superior fatigue allowables. This is not a simple material substitution; it is a major design change that affects:

**Primary Scope Elements:**
- Complete structural redesign of the Main Rotor Hub Assembly (AF-MAAP1-RS-1001-00), generating a new part number (AF-MAAP1-RS-1001-01 or successor dash number)
- New Ti-6Al-4V forging die design and fabrication for the revised hub geometry
- Full building-block qualification test program (coupon, element, component, full-scale) using the new design
- Updated fatigue and damage tolerance analysis using titanium material allowables and new forging geometry
- Revised machining program, tooling, and process qualification for the new hub geometry
- Updated First Article Inspection (FAI) requirements per AS9102 for the new configuration
- Supplier qualification or re-qualification actions for the forging and machining supply chain

**Secondary Scope Elements Requiring Assessment:**
- Galvanic compatibility review and potential material upgrades for the Scissors Assembly (AF-MAAP1-RS-1011-00, currently Al 7075-T7351, $28,000) and Pitch Control Horn assemblies (AF-MAAP1-RS-1004-00, Al 7075-T7351, $18,500 × 5 per aircraft), both of which are in direct structural contact with the titanium hub
- Thermal expansion coefficient mismatch analysis for the elastomeric bearing interfaces (titanium CTE approximately 5.0 µin/in/°F versus aluminum at 13.1 µin/in/°F), with potential revision of bearing preload, retention fitting design, and replacement procedures
- Rotor dynamics re-analysis for the revised hub mass and stiffness properties, including blade natural frequency verification against the 1.15/rev ± 0.05 specification and ground/air resonance margin assessment
- Vibration survey re-baseline against the ≤0.50 in/sec RMS specification at rotor hubs
- Updated Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) for rotor speed governing and HAZ-016 (rotor overspeed/structural failure) to reflect revised material properties and structural margins
- Rotor track and balance re-demonstration to the ±0.5 in blade tip tracking tolerance
- Transmissions interface loads review for any changes in mast load path stiffness resulting from titanium's higher elastic modulus (~116 GPa versus aluminum's ~69 GPa)

**Out of Scope — Unless Secondary Analysis Reveals Necessity:**
- Main transmission redesign (RISK-T-002 contingency monitor only)
- Tail Rotor Hub (AF-MAAP1-RS-2001-00, currently Al 7075-T7351 — separate CCB action if warranted)
- Rotor blade design changes (blade grips already specified as Ti-6Al-4V; blade bodies unaffected)
- Avionics, autonomous systems, or mission system changes

### 2.3 Common Configuration Boundary Condition

Because the main rotor hub is a Green Aircraft common component, every element of scope described above applies simultaneously and identically to all three variants (MAAP-1F, MAAP-1I, MAAP-1C). There is no mechanism within current program policy to implement a material change on one variant without applying it to all, absent a waiver from the Chief Engineer and Program Executive Officer. The future MAAP-1N (Naval) variant should also be assessed for hub design compatibility at this time, as the naval environment's salt-air corrosion profile makes titanium's inherent corrosion resistance particularly advantageous and may justify earlier commitment to the titanium design for that derivative program.

### 2.4 BOM Discrepancy — Mandatory Scope Item

Regardless of which interpretation governs the operative scenario, the following configuration management action is mandatory scope for this change action and must be completed before any downstream program action:

An emergency engineering and finance review must be convened within ten business days of this PMP Update's issuance to formally determine whether the current cost baseline reflects titanium or aluminum hub pricing. The outcome of that review determines the class and scope of the change action. If the BOM is already titanium-priced and the design is mature, the change request should be closed with a configuration management discrepancy notice. If the BOM is aluminum-era pricing carried forward, the cost baseline must be corrected as a prerequisite to any financial decision. This review is a prerequisite gate that cannot be bypassed.

## 4. Change Management Plan

### 4.1 Change Classification and Governance

The proposed replacement of the main rotor hub is formally classified as a **Class I High-Impact Engineering Change** under the program's change classification framework. This classification is established by the following criteria, all of which are met:

- **Cost threshold exceeded:** Total program cost impact estimated at $88M–$105M (central estimate ~$94M), far exceeding the $500,000 Class I threshold.
- **New tooling fabrication required:** New Ti-6Al-4V forging dies, machining fixtures, and inspection tooling are required.
- **Supplier qualification required:** New or re-qualification of the titanium forging and machining supply chain is required.
- **Multi-variant impact:** The hub is a Green Aircraft common component affecting all three variants simultaneously.
- **Safety-critical component:** The hub is an explicitly named Safety-Critical Items List (SCIL) item under SRD-HAZ-006.
- **Schedule impact exceeds 8 weeks:** Critical path impact estimated at 3–9 months depending on timing.

### 4.2 Change Control Board Composition and Responsibilities

The CCB for this change shall include, at minimum, the following members with the following roles:

| Member | Role | Authority |
|---|---|---|
| Program Manager (Chair) | Decision authority for Class I changes | Approve, reject, or defer with conditions |
| Chief Engineer | Technical disposition and design authority | Technical go/no-go recommendation |
| VP Operations | Manufacturing and supply chain feasibility | Resource commitment authority |
| VP Finance / Program Controller | Cost and margin disposition | Management reserve authorization |
| Program Executive Officer | Common configuration waiver co-authority | Required signatory for Green Aircraft change |
| Supply Chain Director | Procurement and supplier commitment | Sole-source and advance payment authorization |
| Manufacturing Engineering Manager | Process qualification and tooling feasibility | Manufacturing readiness disposition |
| Government Program Representative (if applicable) | Class I change concurrence | Customer equitable adjustment position |

### 4.3 ECP Content Requirements

The Engineering Change Proposal submitted to the CCB must contain the following elements, all of which must be complete before the CCB convenes for disposition:

1. **BOM Discrepancy Resolution Memorandum** — signed by Chief Engineer and Controller, confirming whether the current cost baseline reflects aluminum or titanium hub pricing and the disposition of the configuration management discrepancy.

2. **Technical Trade Study** — 30-day structural analysis comparing (a) redesigned titanium hub, (b) current aluminum hub with fatigue enhancement (shot peening, cold working, surface treatment), and (c) current BOM titanium hub as-is. Must include quantified weight delta, fatigue life improvement, and cost-per-fatigue-life-hour metric for each option.

3. **Cross-Variant Impact Assessment** — per PRB Section 5.1 waiver requirements, addressing cost, schedule, and logistics footprint for MAAP-1F, MAAP-1I, MAAP-1C, and prospective MAAP-1N.

4. **Class I vs. Class II Classification Determination** — formal assessment of whether the fatigue life improvement addresses a government-identified deficiency (Class I, customer-fundable) or is an internally initiated improvement (Class II, internal absorption). This determination directly controls whether the $94M central cost estimate is a contract modification candidate or a management reserve draw.

5. **Should-Cost Estimate** — detailed bottom-up cost estimate for NRE (forging die, tooling, qualification testing, engineering redesign) and recurring unit cost delta, with confidence bounds and basis of estimate.

6. **Schedule Impact Analysis** — IMS-referenced impact assessment identifying critical path effects, float consumption, and milestone dates at risk, with and without the block-change alternative.

7. **Risk Assessment** — all new and elevated risks identified in this PMP Update, with initial likelihood and consequence scores.

8. **Mitigation Portfolio** — quantified mitigation options as specified in Section 4.4 below.

### 4.4 Mitigation Portfolio

The following mitigations must be evaluated and their cost and schedule impacts quantified before CCB disposition:

**Mitigation 1 — Class I Customer Funding Pursuit (Priority: Immediate)**
Engage the customer to determine whether the fatigue life improvement addresses a government-identified specification deficiency. If the original specification underspecified fatigue life and the customer agrees, the change becomes a Class I Engineering Change with potential for equitable adjustment. Even 50% customer cost recovery would reduce internal exposure from $94M to approximately $47M — a program-preserving outcome on an FFP contract. The Program Manager and Contracts officer must assess the customer relationship and contractual basis for this action before the CCB convenes.

**Mitigation 2 — Aluminum Fatigue Enhancement Alternative (Priority: Immediate)**
Commission a 30-day structural trade study comparing the titanium redesign against an aluminum hub with fatigue life enhancement techniques (shot peening, cold working of fastener holes, hard anodize, surface treatment). If the aluminum alternative can achieve 80% or more of the fatigue life improvement at 20% or less of the cost, this is the preferred path. The NRE for an aluminum fatigue enhancement program is estimated at less than $5M versus the $26M central NRE estimate for titanium — a compelling cost-risk tradeoff that must be formally evaluated before committing to titanium.

**Mitigation 3 — Leverage Existing Titanium Capability (Priority: If Change Approved)**
The program already produces titanium components (engine mounts per BOM, blade leading edge erosion strips). Leveraging existing NADCAP-certified titanium machining processes and qualified suppliers for the hub program can reduce NRE by an estimated $1.5M–$3M relative to treating titanium hub machining as an entirely new process establishment.

**Mitigation 4 — Sole-Source Supplier with Multi-Year Fixed-Price Commitment (Priority: If Change Approved)**
Negotiate a multi-year fixed-price forging agreement with a single qualified titanium forging supplier before the hub design is finalized, capturing pricing before the supplier recognizes their leverage. This approach, analogous to the GE engine strategy referenced in the financial analysis, can reduce material cost growth risk over the program life by an estimated $15M–$25M versus spot market procurement.

**Mitigation 5 — Combined Qualification Testing (Priority: If Change Approved)**
Pursue combination of hub qualification testing with the existing DTA-1 structural test program rather than standing up a separate qualification article, avoiding an estimated $4M–$7M in incremental test article and test stand cost. This requires schedule alignment and must be assessed against DTA-1 structural test objectives.

**Mitigation 6 — R&D Tax Credit Capture (Priority: If Change Approved)**
All qualifying Ti hub development costs — engineering redesign labor, coupon testing, component qualification — are eligible for the R&D tax credit under IRC §41. Estimated tax benefit: $2M–$4M. The Controller must update the tax credit analysis to include hub redesign NRE.

**Total Mitigation Potential: $22.5M–$39M** — meaningful but insufficient to offset the central $94M program cost impact without customer funding. The CCB must understand this explicitly.

### 4.5 Go/No-Go Decision Framework

| Condition | CCB Recommendation |
|---|---|
| BOM already Ti, design already mature and qualified | No action required — close change request; issue CM discrepancy notice |
| Customer will fund ≥50% via equitable adjustment | Proceed with change; manage residual ~$47M through management reserve and mitigation portfolio |
| Weight penalty exceeds 20 lb net and aluminum + surface treatment achieves fatigue target | Reject titanium change; implement aluminum fatigue enhancement at <$5M NRE |
| Full internal absorption required, no schedule relief, decision after CDR | Reject as Class II (fails margin-neutral threshold); adopt block-change at LRIP-2; escalate to Program VP and customer for scope negotiation |
| Full internal absorption required, decision before CDR, mitigation portfolio activated | Conditional approval with management reserve draw authorization, monthly margin tracking, and go/no-go gate at PM [CDR+4 months] on qualification data availability |

### 4.6 Production Break-In Point

Per the Configuration Management Plan, production changes require a minimum three-month lead time. The titanium hub change requires far greater lead time than the policy minimum. The following production break-in constraints apply:

- If approved before CDR: Target incorporation at DTA-1 fabrication start (Feb 2028); all test articles built to titanium configuration.
- If approved after CDR but before DTA-1 fabrication: Emergency acceleration of forging procurement; potential DTA-1 schedule slip of 2–4 months.
- If approved after DTA-1 fabrication start: Block-change at LRIP-2 is mandatory; aluminum hub completes initial qualification program; titanium hub qualification runs on parallel track.
- All LRIP-1 aircraft built to aluminum hub configuration under block-change scenario must be assessed for retrofit requirement and cost.

The CCB must use the BOM discrepancy resolution to determine which recurring cost scenario is the baseline.

### 5.4 Margin Impact

| Scenario | Cost Increase | Margin Impact (bps) | Resulting Gross Margin |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Conservative (NRE only, BOM already Ti-priced, redesign only)** | ~$21M | −51 bps | 9.4% |
| **Central (NRE + moderate recurring delta)** | ~$52M | −126 bps | 8.6% |
| **Adverse (full NRE + full recurring gap, Interpretation C)** | ~$94M | −228 bps | 7.6% |

The **central scenario alone consumes approximately 70% of the $74M management reserve**. The adverse scenario exhausts the reserve entirely and erodes program margin below levels that historically trigger corporate-level review. Under the program's change control policy, Class II changes are "approved only if margin-neutral or positive." No scenario presented above meets this threshold. This is the strongest programmatic argument against approving this change as an internally absorbed Class II action.

### 5.5 Cash Flow Implications

The following near-term cash outflows are associated with the proposed change during the development phase (PM 0–48):

| Item | Timing | Cash Impact |
|---|---|---|
| Engineering redesign labor | PM 1–12 | −$1.5M to −$6.5M |
| Ti forging die development and machining tooling | PM 6–18 | −$0.95M to −$7.5M |
